content.text: Bug Severity and Priority Matrix for AI Issues

Triaging bugs in an AI application requires two independent assessments: how severe is the defect in terms of its nature and impact, and how urgently should it be fixed relative to everything else in the backlog. Conflating these two dimensions produces misallocation — a severe-but-narrow bug fixed before a moderate-but-widespread one, or a low-severity cosmetic issue that sits in the backlog untouched for a year while engineers could have fixed it in an afternoon. The matrix separates severity (a property of the bug itself) from priority (a scheduling decision shaped by severity, scope, and product context).

AI-specific bugs introduce failure modes that do not map cleanly onto the severity scales designed for conventional software. A model producing outputs with subtly degraded quality is not a "crash" or a "data loss" event, yet it may affect every user of the application. A safety violation that occurs on one in ten thousand requests is low-frequency but extremely high severity. The matrix must account for these AI-specific dimensions — output quality, safety, and probabilistic failure rates — alongside the infrastructure dimensions that traditional severity models cover.

attrs.alt: Bug severity and priority matrix showing P0–P3 triage outcomes across severity levels and customer impact scopes

content.text: Severity Definitions

Severity describes the nature and intrinsic impact of the defect, independent of how many customers have reported it. A bug is SEV-1 regardless of whether one customer or a thousand has encountered it, if its nature is critical. Severity does not change based on customer tier or commercial importance — those factors influence priority, not severity. Mixing commercial context into severity creates a system where engineers cannot predict how a newly discovered bug will be classified, because the classification depends on business factors rather than technical ones.

from enum import Enum, auto

class Severity(int, Enum):
    SEV1_CRITICAL  = 1   # data loss, safety violation, complete service unavailability
    SEV2_HIGH      = 2   # significant functional degradation, security vulnerability
    SEV3_MEDIUM    = 3   # feature impaired; workaround exists
    SEV4_LOW       = 4   # minor functional issue; no user impact on typical paths
    SEV5_COSMETIC  = 5   # visual/text defect; no functional impact

# AI-specific severity anchors
AI_SEVERITY_ANCHORS = {
    Severity.SEV1_CRITICAL: [
        "Model produces output that violates safety guidelines (harmful content, PII exfiltration)",
        "Model returns another user's conversation content (data exposure)",
        "Inference endpoint completely unavailable for all requests",
        "Model outputs cause customer application to make irreversible incorrect decisions (e.g. financial transactions)",
    ],
    Severity.SEV2_HIGH: [
        "Output quality degraded > 20% on primary task metrics (measured, not perceived)",
        "Model refuses all requests for a specific prompt pattern used by > 10% of users",
        "Context window corruption: model ignores system prompt instructions",
        "Latency regression > 3× baseline at P95 for a model tier",
    ],
    Severity.SEV3_MEDIUM: [
        "Output quality degraded 5–20% on secondary task metrics",
        "Model occasionally refuses valid requests (< 5% refusal rate on affected pattern)",
        "Rate limiting stricter than documented; workaround available via retry",
        "Streaming output drops tokens intermittently; full response correct on retry",
    ],
    Severity.SEV4_LOW: [
        "Output formatting inconsistent with documented spec in edge cases",
        "Model version pinning behaves unexpectedly on version boundaries",
        "SDK error message text is misleading but error handling is correct",
    ],
    Severity.SEV5_COSMETIC: [
        "Documentation typo or inaccuracy with no functional consequence",
        "Dashboard label truncated on narrow viewports",
        "API response includes undocumented field with no customer impact",
    ],
}
Priority Definitions and SLA Targets

Priority is the scheduling classification that determines when engineering work begins on a bug. It is derived from severity combined with impact scope — how many customers are affected and whether enterprise contractual obligations are at risk. The same SEV-2 bug is P0 if it affects all customers and P2 if it affects a single customer, because the scope determines the urgency of the fix relative to everything else in the backlog.
Priority
SLA to Assign Owner
SLA to First Fix Attempt
Escalation if Missed
P0
Immediate (15 min)
1 hour
VP Eng paged; incident declared
P1
2 hours
Same business day (4h)
EM notified; moved to current sprint if not already
P2
1 business day
72 hours
EM reviews at sprint planning
P3
Within 1 week
No SLA — backlog
Reviewed at quarterly backlog hygiene

from dataclasses import dataclass

class Priority(int, Enum):
    P0 = 0  # immediate — drop everything
    P1 = 1  # same-day
    P2 = 2  # this sprint
    P3 = 3  # backlog

class ImpactScope(str, Enum):
    ALL_CUSTOMERS      = "all_customers"
    ENTERPRISE_ONLY    = "enterprise_only"
    SUBSET_OF_USERS    = "subset_of_users"
    SINGLE_CUSTOMER    = "single_customer"
    INTERNAL_ONLY      = "internal_only"

# Priority lookup: (Severity, ImpactScope) → Priority
PRIORITY_MATRIX: dict[tuple[Severity, ImpactScope], Priority] = {
    (Severity.SEV1_CRITICAL,  ImpactScope.ALL_CUSTOMERS):   Priority.P0,
    (Severity.SEV1_CRITICAL,  ImpactScope.ENTERPRISE_ONLY): Priority.P0,
    (Severity.SEV1_CRITICAL,  ImpactScope.SUBSET_OF_USERS): Priority.P1,
    (Severity.SEV1_CRITICAL,  ImpactScope.SINGLE_CUSTOMER): Priority.P2,
    (Severity.SEV2_HIGH,      ImpactScope.ALL_CUSTOMERS):   Priority.P0,
    (Severity.SEV2_HIGH,      ImpactScope.ENTERPRISE_ONLY): Priority.P1,
    (Severity.SEV2_HIGH,      ImpactScope.SUBSET_OF_USERS): Priority.P2,
    (Severity.SEV2_HIGH,      ImpactScope.SINGLE_CUSTOMER): Priority.P3,
    (Severity.SEV3_MEDIUM,    ImpactScope.ALL_CUSTOMERS):   Priority.P1,
    (Severity.SEV3_MEDIUM,    ImpactScope.ENTERPRISE_ONLY): Priority.P2,
    (Severity.SEV3_MEDIUM,    ImpactScope.SUBSET_OF_USERS): Priority.P3,
    (Severity.SEV3_MEDIUM,    ImpactScope.SINGLE_CUSTOMER): Priority.P3,
    (Severity.SEV4_LOW,       ImpactScope.ALL_CUSTOMERS):   Priority.P1,
    (Severity.SEV4_LOW,       ImpactScope.ENTERPRISE_ONLY): Priority.P2,
    (Severity.SEV4_LOW,       ImpactScope.SUBSET_OF_USERS): Priority.P3,
    (Severity.SEV4_LOW,       ImpactScope.SINGLE_CUSTOMER): Priority.P3,
    (Severity.SEV5_COSMETIC,  ImpactScope.ALL_CUSTOMERS):   Priority.P2,
    # All other cosmetic combinations → P3
}

def triage(severity: Severity, scope: ImpactScope) -> Priority:
    return PRIORITY_MATRIX.get((severity, scope), Priority.P3)
AI-Specific Triage Adjustments

Three categories of AI bugs require triage adjustments that the base matrix does not capture: safety violations, probabilistic failures, and quality regression without a hard failure signal. These categories have specific override rules that raise priority above what the matrix would otherwise assign.
Safety violations are always P0, regardless of scope.

A safety violation affects a single customer at a rate of one request in a million is still P0. The reason is not the frequency of occurrence — it is the nature of the defect and the regulatory and reputational exposure it creates. A safety violation that is "only" SEV-2 in scope receives a mandatory priority uplift to P0. The Policy team and the Responsible Scaling team are notified on triage, not on fix.
Probabilistic failures are triaged at their worst-case scope.

A bug that fails 3% of requests of a specific type is not a "single-customer" bug — if the request type is common, the effective scope is "all customers of that type." Triage must estimate the population of affected users from request logs before assigning scope, not from the number of customer reports. The number of reports is a lagging indicator of scope.
Quality regressions without hard errors require a quality score baseline.

A model quality regression does not produce an error code or a stack trace. It produces outputs that are subtly worse than the previous model version on a task the customer cares about. To triage these bugs, the team needs an established quality baseline for the task. Without a baseline, "the model is worse" is not a bug report — it is an observation. Quality bugs must include a reproducible evaluation showing the delta from baseline before they can be triaged.
Triage Meeting Cadence and Ownership

Triage is not a one-time action at bug creation. Priority can change as more data arrives — the initial scope estimate from one customer report may be revised when log analysis shows the bug is wider. A bug triaged as P2 on Monday may be P0 by Wednesday when the error rate data comes in. The triage cadence must match the rate at which bug scope can change.

P0 and P1 bugs are re-assessed in real time — the assigned engineer updates the bug with scope and impact data as it becomes available, and escalates if the picture worsens. P2 bugs are reviewed in a daily triage meeting (15 minutes; support EM + on-call engineer). P3 bugs are reviewed at weekly bug triage (30 minutes; full engineering team). The weekly triage is also where newly filed bugs that arrived without complete information are assessed and either promoted, demoted, or returned for more data.
Backlog Priority Decay and Hygiene

P3 bugs in the backlog have no fix SLA, but they are not free to accumulate indefinitely. A backlog with 400 P3 bugs has no useful signal — the oldest ones were filed against a model version that no longer exists, the highest-priority ones are indistinguishable from the lowest because everything is P3, and engineers cannot make meaningful decisions about what to work on from the backlog alone. Priority decay and quarterly hygiene reviews keep the backlog actionable.

Priority decay works as follows: a P3 bug that has not been touched in 90 days receives an automated review flag. At the quarterly backlog hygiene session, the team reviews all flagged bugs and makes one of three decisions: close as won't fix (the issue is no longer relevant or has been superseded), promote to P2 (a product change has made it more important than when it was filed), or keep at P3 with an explicit rationale note. Bugs without a rationale note are closed at the next hygiene cycle.
Won't fix criteria for AI bugs:

The model version the bug was filed against has been deprecated; the behavior was intentional and documented; the fix would require a model retrain that is not planned; the customer who reported it is no longer a customer.
Promotion to P2 criteria:

A second enterprise customer has reported the same issue; a feature launch has increased the affected user population; a monitoring alert has started firing on the same behavior; the bug has appeared in a public status page incident.
Backlog health metric:

P3 bug count should not exceed 2× the team's average sprint velocity in story points. A team that ships 40 points per sprint should have no more than ~80 P3 bugs. Beyond that, the backlog is a ledger of abandoned intentions, not a prioritized work queue.
Writing a Triageable Bug Report

A bug report that cannot be triaged is a bug report that sits unclassified until someone has time to investigate it enough to assign it. The triage team should be able to read a bug report and produce a severity and priority classification without any additional investigation. That means the bug report must supply the information the triage matrix requires: the defect description, the reproduction steps, the impact scope estimate with log evidence, and — for AI-specific bugs — the affected model version and a sample request/response.

Support agents filing bugs from customer tickets are the primary source of untriageable reports. They are focused on the customer's problem, not on providing complete technical context. The bug filing form must require the fields the triage team needs and hard-block submission when critical fields are empty. Optional fields produce optional filling-in; required fields produce the data.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class BugReportDraft:
    title: str
    defect_description: str
    steps_to_reproduce: list[str]
    expected_behavior: str
    actual_behavior: str
    # Impact evidence
    affected_user_count_estimate: int       # from logs; 0 if unknown
    customer_tier: str                      # "free", "pro", "enterprise"
    # AI-specific fields
    model_id: str | None                    # e.g. "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022"
    api_endpoint: str | None                # e.g. "/v1/messages"
    sample_prompt: str | None               # sanitised — no PII
    sample_response: str | None             # actual output
    temperature: float | None
    max_tokens: int | None
    is_safety_related: bool = False
    quality_eval_score_before: float | None = None  # pre-regression baseline
    quality_eval_score_after: float | None = None   # post-regression observation

    def validation_errors(self) -> list[str]:
        errors = []
        if not self.steps_to_reproduce:
            errors.append("steps_to_reproduce is required")
        if not self.actual_behavior:
            errors.append("actual_behavior is required")
        if self.model_id is None:
            errors.append("model_id is required for AI bug reports")
        if self.sample_prompt is None or self.sample_response is None:
            errors.append("sample_prompt and sample_response are required for AI bug reports")
        if self.is_safety_related and not self.sample_prompt:
            errors.append("safety-related bugs must include the triggering prompt (sanitised)")
        if (self.quality_eval_score_before is not None) != (self.quality_eval_score_after is not None):
            errors.append("quality_eval_score_before and after must both be set or both be None")
        return errors
Relationship to Incident Severity

Bug severity and incident severity share vocabulary but are not the same classification. An incident has a P0/P1/P2 severity that describes the operational impact during the incident window — how many users are affected right now, whether the service is degraded or unavailable. A bug has a SEV-1 through SEV-5 severity that describes the nature and potential impact of the defect.

The relationship between the two is: incidents often produce bugs, and the bug severity is typically informed by the incident severity — a P0 incident that exposed a data vulnerability produces a SEV-1 bug. But the reverse is not always true: a SEV-1 bug may not have caused a detectable incident yet if the failure mode is low-probability. Bug triage must not wait for an incident to establish severity; it must assess severity from the bug's nature, even when no incident has occurred. This is especially important for security and safety bugs, which are SEV-1 regardless of whether they have been exploited.
Incident Severity
Typical Bug Severity Range
Note
P0 (critical)
SEV-1 to SEV-2
Root cause bug inherits P0 priority
P1 (high)
SEV-2 to SEV-3
Bug priority set by matrix — not inherited from incident
No incident (proactive find)
Any SEV
Severity set purely from defect nature; still SEV-1 for safety/security issues
Triage Disputes and Override Process

Triage decisions will sometimes be disputed. A customer success manager may argue that a P2 bug affecting a single enterprise customer should be P0 because of the commercial relationship. A product manager may argue that a SEV-2 quality regression is actually SEV-3 because the affected task is not the primary use case. These disputes are expected and legitimate — the triage matrix is a default framework, not an infallible oracle.

The override process is structured to prevent ad-hoc re-prioritization from undermining the system. Any triage decision can be escalated to the engineering manager within 24 hours of the initial triage. The EM reviews the dispute and either confirms or changes the decision with a written rationale. The rationale is recorded in the bug ticket. If the same category of dispute recurs more than three times in a quarter, the triage criteria themselves are reviewed and updated — the matrix should reflect the judgements the team actually makes, not an aspirational policy that gets overridden repeatedly.
Valid override grounds:

The original triage used incorrect scope data; the bug involves a safety or security dimension that was not identified at filing; a contractual obligation requires faster resolution than the matrix assigns; a product change has materially increased the impact scope since filing.
Invalid override grounds:

The customer is commercially important (priority, not severity, handles this via enterprise SLA); the reporter has a strong opinion; the bug is easy to fix (ease of fix is not a triage criterion — it is a scheduling consideration that may be noted in the ticket but does not change priority); the bug was filed by an executive.
Tracking Triage Accuracy Over Time

The triage matrix produces predictions about how urgently a bug needs to be fixed. Those predictions can be validated retrospectively: did bugs triaged as P2 actually turn out to require P0 urgency because scope expanded? Did bugs triaged as P0 turn out to be narrow issues that could have waited? This retrospective validation is how the matrix improves. Without it, the matrix reflects the team's initial assumptions about bug severity and scope, which may be systematically off in specific categories.

Triage accuracy is measured on a quarterly basis. For each bug resolved in the quarter, the final recorded scope and customer impact is compared to the scope estimated at triage. Bugs where the scope grew by more than one tier (single-customer → all-customers) indicate that the initial scope estimation process for that bug category is too optimistic. Bugs where scope contracted significantly indicate over-triage. Both patterns are inputs to the next quarterly review of the severity anchors and the matrix defaults.

An under-triage rate above 10% indicates the team is systematically underestimating the scope of certain bug categories. An over-triage rate above 15% indicates the team is over-escalating, which causes priority inflation — when too many bugs are P1, P1 loses its meaning as a scheduling signal. Both thresholds trigger a review of the specific bug categories contributing to the error before the next quarterly cycle.
Summary

The bug severity and priority matrix separates the intrinsic nature of a defect (severity) from its scheduling urgency (priority). For AI applications, three categories require special handling: safety violations are unconditionally P0 regardless of scope, probabilistic failures are scoped from log data rather than report count, and quality regressions require a measured quality delta before triage can proceed. The matrix produces consistent, predictable triage decisions that allocate engineering attention to the bugs that matter most without relying on commercial pressure or customer escalation to drive the classification.
